actStructure:
**PROLOGUE — "The 11:47."** A night train that never stops, stops. You step off into Foley. At Cade's Diner, the town welcomes Wren home, and character creation happens diegetically: neighbors reminisce — "Wren, you remember how you always—" — and the PLAYER completes their sentences (LLM touchpoint 1). The town warms or squints at the answers; stats seed from them. Prologue ends with the first chalk X discovered on the milliner's door, and the milliner gone by morning. Only you remember her.

**ACT 1 — "Roll Call."** Learn the town's rhythm: Sunday roll call at the bandstand, Gil's mail route, the exchange where Mercy patches your calls (saves). Beatrice Alder starts thinning — her motif loses its cello. The player-facing mystery ignites: people are being erased and the town's memory closes over them like water — except yours doesn't. **BRANCH A (soft divergence):** at roll call, Odile's cover of a missing part audibly fails. Expose the gap publicly, help her paper over it, or stay silent. This sets which of Odile/Gil becomes your Act 2 confidant, whether the town knows it's dying, and whether Beatrice can be Steadied in front of everyone or must be saved in secret.

**ACT 2 — "Thin Places."** Three investigation strands the player orders freely, each gated differently: Gil's undelivered mailbag (Regard+low Trespass path or high-Trespass break-in), Pinch's expedition to the hem (requires keeping Pinch's secret; Static draws Pinch to you), and the Registry basement beneath the exchange (Trespass-gated; terminal-flavored even in browser). **BRANCH B (hard divergence):** Harrow offers the Bargain — he will pass over someone you love if you name another "for the survey." ACCEPT: the Erasure track opens, the ambient score starts losing instruments town-wide, and Act 3 arrives with a smaller, frightened cast; Survey Complete arms. REFUSE: Harrow chalks YOUR door; the town rallies to scrub it or doesn't, depending on Regard — a public referendum on whether you're really theirs. Mid-act false exit: board the 11:47 and get the Wrong Number ending. Act 2 closes when any strand surfaces the flood: photos, notices, or the hem's water-sky.

**ACT 3 — "The Signature."** The Registry basement yields the Final Disconnection Order, signed W. Vasco — your signature. The reveal sequence replays the prologue diner scene as what it truly was: the archive interviewing a stranger to patch its Wren-shaped hole, your own typed words read back from the census ledger in the town's voice. Mercy, at last, tells you about the promise: Wren said they'd call when it was safe to hang up. She thinks the call finally came. **BRANCH C (terminal divergence):** countersign the order (THE LAST ROLL CALL), burn it (OPEN LINE), carry the town through the hem instead (THE HEM — only if Pinch's route stayed open), complete the forgery of yourself (THE UNDERSTUDY — high Standing/Trespass, Gil's stamps), or, if the Bargains ran deep, Harrow simply finishes (SURVEY COMPLETE). World-state entering Act 3 — who is still whole, who is thin, who was named — was set at Branch B, so the same Act 3 choice plays against very different towns.

**ACT 4 — Finale variants** per ending, each built around a different resolution of the main theme's missing note.

cast.name: Mercy Olm — the Night Operator
cast.role: Keeper of the Foley telephone exchange; the save system made flesh. Every save is diegetic — she 'holds your call.' She is the one entity aware of the player: she never says Wren, only 'caller,' and asks about the weather where YOU are.
cast.want: To never miss a call. She has kept one line open for seventy years because Wren promised to call when it was safe to hang up.
cast.secret: The Final Disconnection Order — the instruction to shut the exchange and end Foley — has sat on her desk since 1954. She has refused it every night. And she is the reason you're here: she never stopped leaving the line open for anyone outside to pick up.
cast.reactsToMemory: Her line-checks track your accumulated honesty. If you've lied to residents, she stops verifying your calls — 'I'll just patch you through. No questions tonight' — clipped, wounded, professional. If you've kept promises, she starts small talk across the wire, and by Act 3 she asks the question she's held for decades: 'Caller… is it safe to hang up yet?' Reload often and she greets you with 'Still there? You keep dropping,' and your Static rises — other residents notice the fuzz on you.

cast.name: Odile Bell — the Bandmaster
cast.role: Conducts the Sunday roll call, where the town hums itself whole — Foley's integrity check, disguised as tradition. Guardian of the master score, i.e., the census itself.
cast.want: One perfect roll call: every part present, every note accounted for.
cast.secret: Parts have been going missing for years. She has been transposing the holes herself — covering the dying with her own arrangements so the town won't panic. And she has known since your first Sunday that your part isn't merely missing from her book. There was never a line for you at all.
cast.reactsToMemory: If you helped her cover the failing roll call in Act 1, she eventually opens the master score to you — the single best clue source in the game. If you exposed her, she rewrites her arrangements to exclude you: in any scene she's in, your accompaniment literally drops out of the mix, and players with high Attunement can hear themselves being erased from the town's music.

cast.name: Gil Marchetti — the Postman
cast.role: Sorts and re-delivers the town's mail on a 37-year rotation, because nothing new ever arrives. The town's clock, and its conscience.
cast.want: One new letter. Just one envelope he hasn't memorized.
cast.secret: In 1953 he received the county's evacuation notices — and never delivered them. He believes the flood is his fault; that if the town had 'left properly,' it wouldn't be trapped like this. The undelivered mailbag is under the sorting-room floor, and inside it is the paper trail to the dam, the reservoir, and the date everything stopped.
cast.reactsToMemory: Gil watches how you treat sealed things. Open other people's letters, jimmy other people's doors, and he re-hides the bag and changes his route around you. Respect every seal you meet and he brings the bag to YOU in Act 2, unasked, saying 'I figure you're the only one who'd read these and still stay.' It's the game's clearest lesson that Trespass is remembered even when it isn't witnessed.

cast.name: Sam 'Pinch' Duran — the Kid at the Edges
cast.role: Eleven years old, maps the town's borders in a composition notebook. The route to the third ending.
cast.want: To see the ocean. (Foley is landlocked. The water is above.)
cast.secret: Pinch has touched the hem — the town's edge, where streets dissolve into static and the sound of deep water — and brought back a living fish. Worse: Pinch died before the flood (the one grave the county moved), so Pinch's census entry was reconstructed from other people's memories, not a recording. Pinch is made of what Foley remembers about a child — and flickers.
cast.reactsToMemory: Pinch is drawn to Static — the more the outside clings to you (reloads, refused rituals, out-of-place words), the more Pinch trusts you: 'You buzz like the hem does.' Keep Pinch's fish and maps secret and the Act 3 hem route stays open. Tell any adult and Pinch stops mapping, goes home, and the notebook page for you is torn out — permanently closing THE HEM ending for that run.

cast.name: Mr. Harrow — the Surveyor
cast.role: The county surveyor who condemned Foley in '53, still walking his measurement lines, chalking X's on doors. He is the archive's deletion routine, wearing the memory of the most feared man in town. Antagonist — but not a villain.
cast.want: Completion of the survey. Every parcel assessed, every degraded entry cleared.
cast.secret: Two layers: his chalk marks are triage, not malice — he deletes the unrecoverable so corruption doesn't spread to everyone. And the order he serves — the reveal's hinge — bears the census-taker's signature. Wren's. Yours. Harrow isn't defying the town's maker; he is obeying them.
cast.reactsToMemory: Harrow's register tracks yours. Refuse his Bargain and protect the thin, and he stays icy county-formal: 'You are an irregularity, and irregularities are surveyed last.' Let residents fade when you could have Steadied them, or accept a Bargain, and his tone warms horribly — he starts calling you 'colleague,' shows you his chalk, explains his triage math like a mentor. Players report this as the most upsetting kindness in the game.

cast.name: Beatrice Alder — the Librarian
cast.role: First of the thinning. She teaches the player — and the player's ear — what dying sounds like here: her leitmotif loses its cello in Act 1, then its left hand.
cast.want: To finish the water-damaged shelf she has been rereading for seventy years, because she can no longer hold new pages in her memory.
cast.secret: She knows exactly what is happening to her, and has been leaving goodbye notes as marginalia in library books — indexed, in her own catalog system, so the town will find them one at a time, gently, after she's gone.
cast.reactsToMemory: She is the tutorial for the Steadying mechanic and the game's long memory made visible: hum her motif back with a true memory of her and she stabilizes, survives to Act 3, and hands you the library's flood photograph — Foley's main street under forty feet of green water. Fail her, or feed her a false memory (the score answers with a dissonant sting), and hers is the next chalked door; every later library visit plays the ambient theme with a cello-shaped hole in it.

cluesInRetrospect: 1) **Track 00: [UNRECORDED].** The diner jukebox and the exchange's music cabinet list every resident's theme by census number — playable from hour one. Your slot, Track 00, is thirty seconds of tape hiss titled [UNRECORDED]. Players read it as a stylish 'silent protagonist' joke. It is the literal hole in the archive you were poured into. 2) **No first meeting.** No one can tell a story about meeting you; every anecdote about Wren resolves onto someone else mid-telling ('you were there— no, wait, that was Odile'). Reads as small-town memory fuzz; it is the town auto-repairing around a vacancy. 3) **The dates never move.** Diner newspapers say June 1954; Gil's 'new' letters carry postmarks in exact 37-year cycles; the exchange calendar shows seventy years of the same page. Browser players see faded, easy-to-miss dates; terminal players see raw Registry timestamps frozen at 1954-06-12 with a separate 'elapsed' counter running — stronger in terminal, present in both, required in neither. 4) **The lake in the sky.** At every edge of town you hear deep water ABOVE you — rain that never lands, kids joking about the 'upstairs ocean,' a live fish in the church font. Foley is at the bottom of the reservoir, and the game never hides it; it just lets you assume it's mood. 5) **Mercy's grammar.** She alone never says your name — only 'caller' — asks what the weather is like 'where you are' (Foley has one weather), and answers the save prompt 'Hold the line?' The save system was a character the whole time. 6) **You are never shown Wren's face.** Mirrors are steamed, cracked, 'the light's bad'; the census has photographs of everyone but its taker; character creation is framed as neighbors reminiscing rather than you describing yourself. At the reveal, the prologue diner scene replays verbatim with one change of camera meaning: the town wasn't welcoming Wren home. It was interviewing whoever picked up the phone.
emotionalCore: The feeling: **being held in memory by people you will never touch — grief experienced as gratitude.** 'Someone kept the line open for you for seventy years.' Every system serves this: saves are Mercy holding your call, music is how the town holds its people, stats are the shape of how a town holds a stranger. The scene everything is built toward is THE LAST ROLL CALL: the town gathers at the bandstand at dusk, and one by one each character's leitmotif — the same fragments you've heard degrade, transpose, and thin for ten hours — is finally played WHOLE, once, while they look toward the bandstand microphone (toward the line, toward you) and say some version of thank you to a caller they never met. Beatrice's cello comes back for her last sixteen bars. Pinch's whistle finally lands on the downbeat. The main theme's missing note is filled by every voice at once. And then Mercy, alone in the exchange, the last light in a drowned town, says 'It was good to hear your voice, caller,' and — for the first time in seventy years — hangs up. The screen doesn't fade. The line just goes quiet, and the player is left holding a phone in an empty room, which is where they have been all along. If we execute nothing else, we execute this scene.

endings.name: The Last Roll Call
endings.howReached: Learn the full truth (order + signature + flood), keep the majority of the cast whole through Act 2, earn Mercy's trust (high Regard, honest line-checks), then countersign Wren's order WITH her rather than over her. Requires having Steadied at least one resident — you must understand what you're releasing.
endings.tone: Devastating and luminous — the money scene. Every leitmotif completed in full at the bandstand as each resident ends; Mercy disconnects last, thanking the caller; the main theme's missing note is finally sung by everyone at once.

endings.name: Open Line
endings.howReached: Learn the truth and burn the order. Foley persists; Harrow's triage continues; you choose stasis with open eyes because you cannot end them — or because Mercy, if her trust is low, begs you not to make her hang up.
endings.tone: Bittersweet stasis curdling gently at the edges. Final shot: Mercy re-lighting the switchboard for another night, 'in case you call again.' On NG+ her first line is 'You again. The line remembers.'

endings.name: The Hem
endings.howReached: Keep Pinch's secrets all game, recover Gil's mailbag, and refuse both the order and the refusal: carry the town OUT through the hem, transcribing who the residents have become — not who they were recorded as — into the save itself. The save file is renamed 'Foley (copy)' and the finale renders the town as living text, walking out of the water, even in the browser build.
endings.tone: Strange, defiant hope. 'Somewhere is better than not here.' Players who have used both platforms on this save get one extra line from Mercy: 'You found both doors. I always wondered who would.'

endings.name: The Understudy
endings.howReached: The seduction path: high Standing and Trespass, Gil's official stamps, Odile's transposed score — forge the census entry and convince Foley you truly ARE Wren. The town's memory heals around the lie. You finally get a leitmotif of your own.
endings.tone: Warm on the surface, wrong in the ear. Your new theme is audibly patchwork — stolen bars of Odile's transpositions — and high-Attunement players (and any player who's studied the jukebox) can HEAR the forgery. The music itself is the unreliable narrator's tell. Track 00 is renamed, but the hiss is still underneath.

endings.name: Survey Complete
endings.howReached: Take Harrow's Bargains, name names, let the thinning fade unSteadied. Harrow finishes the survey with your effective cooperation; Foley ends without music — no roll call, just doors and chalk.
endings.tone: Quiet horror. The final chalk X appears on the exchange door while Mercy is still inside; her last line down the wire is 'Caller… who have I been talking to?' Then dial tone over unlit credits. The soundtrack for the ending is the metronome alone.

endings.name: Wrong Number
endings.howReached: The false exit: board the 11:47 out of Foley in Act 2 before learning what the town is. Available to any player at a clearly marked moment — the game genuinely lets you leave.
endings.tone: Abrupt and haunting; a deliberate replay-magnet. Credits roll over train sound, then are interrupted: a phone ringing somewhere in the mix, and Mercy's voice, small: '…Caller? Are you still there?' Cut to title. The title screen's phone is now off the hook until you start a new run.

llmTouchpoints: Four touchpoints, all narrow, all with authored fallbacks — the LLM never writes canon, it only interprets the player. **(1) 'Remembering Wren' — character creation.** Neighbors reminisce in the prologue diner: 'Wren, remember how you always—' and the player completes 4 sentences in free text. An LLM maps each answer to stat seeds (Regard/Attunement/Trespass/Static) and tags 2–3 short player phrases as CALLBACKS, inserted into pre-authored quote slots later — so in Act 3 the census ledger reads the player's own words back as 'memories' of Wren, which is the reveal's gut-punch. Fallback: curated sentence-completion menus with pre-tagged callbacks; identical downstream pipeline. **(2) Mercy's line-checks.** A few times per act, Mercy asks the CALLER (not Wren) something small and direct — 'It's late where you are, isn't it?' Free-text answers are classified into a tiny enum (warm / guarded / silent / strange) that shifts her disposition and selects among fully authored replies. Player text is never echoed into canon — classification only, so it degrades safely. Fallback: four authored response options. **(3) Steadying — memory verification.** When humming a thinning resident's motif back, the player may offer 'a memory of them' in free text. The LLM checks it against that character's fact sheet: consistent memories stabilize strongly; contradictions trigger a dissonant sting in the score and a weaker hold — the music, not a dialog box, tells you that you got someone wrong. Fallback: choose a memory from journal entries you've actually collected, checked by simple flags. **(4) The Last Log.** In the Roll Call ending only, the player may write the note Mercy files as the exchange's final entry; the LLM formats it into the Registry's typographic house style (spacing, letterhead, no invented content) for a single on-screen appearance. Fallback: an authored template with the Act-1 callbacks slotted in. Total LLM surface: one creation scene, a classifier, a fact-checker, a typesetter. The story is authored; the machine only listens.
logline: A drowned town's telephone exchange has kept one line open for seventy years, waiting for a promised call. Tonight, you picked up — and the town of Foley, where every soul has a song except you, welcomes you home by a name that was never yours.
musicIdentity: **Core conceit: the score IS the mystery — Foley's people are literally made of music.** The town survives as the 'Foley Sonic Census,' recordings made by its choirmaster before the flood, so every leitmotif is diegetic: a person's theme is their soul, their health, and their evidence. **Main theme, 'Not Here':** a warm, hymn-like melody with a deliberate hole — the phrase rests where its resolving note should fall, every time, in every arrangement. The whole town's ambient music is other characters' motifs arranged AROUND that rest. Each ending fills the rest differently: all voices at once (Roll Call), never (Open Line), a naked square-wave — the terminal's voice — completing an orchestral phrase (The Hem, the chills moment), a stolen patchwork phrase (Understudy), a chalk-squeak and silence (Survey). **Leitmotifs:** Mercy = a bare sine tone, like a test signal down a wire, that patiently resolves into a small waltz — her theme always enters through the narrowest timbre and warms, the sound of long distance becoming company. Odile = a brass-band chorale that is slightly over-arranged, doubling parts that shouldn't need doubling (she's covering the dead; the orchestration is her secret). Gil = walking pizzicato bass with paper-shuffle percussion in a 37-bar loop that never quite lines up with the main theme's phrase length (his route, his cycle). Pinch = piccolo/penny-whistle over field-recorded water that never lands on the downbeat — until the endings where Pinch is saved. Harrow = no melody at all: a measured metronome, chalk taps, and a descending whole-tone survey line; when he assesses someone, THEIR motif plays back rigidly quantized — dread by arrangement, not volume. Beatrice = cello and page-turns; her instruments drop out one by one as she thins, the game's audible tutorial in what dying means here. **Adaptive systems:** proximity stem-mixing (nearby characters' stems fade in); thinning characters lose instruments run-wide; the Steadying mechanic has the player hum a motif back (rhythm-matching mini-interaction); the Attunement stat literally widens your mix — high-Attunement players hear a resident fading days before the town does. **Dual-platform rendering, one score:** all music is authored as tracker-style pattern data. The browser renders it through sampled instruments in WebAudio — warm, full, 'standing in the town.' The terminal build renders the SAME patterns through a lo-fi chip synth (sine/square voices via a tiny native audio layer) — thin, distant, 'hearing Foley down the wire,' which is diegetically exactly what the terminal is. With audio unavailable, terminal shows motifs as marginal ASCII notation with named voices, so themes remain legible clues. One melody, two truths — the dual-build requirement becomes the score's central metaphor. **Palette:** small-town Americana chamber band — brass choir, upright piano, cello, penny whistle, pump organ — against telephony textures: sine tones, switchboard clicks, tape hiss, the sounder's rhythm. Hymnal warmth over infrastructure cold.
mystery: As the player lives it, in order: **'Why do I have no song?'** Everyone in Foley carries a personal tune the town plays around them; silence follows you (the jukebox lists your theme as Track 00: [UNRECORDED]). Then: **'Who is erasing people — and why am I the only one who remembers them?'** Residents 'thin' — their leitmotifs shed instruments — until Mr. Harrow chalks an X on their door and they're gone by morning, and the whole town's memory closes over them like water. Except yours. You remember every one of them, which terrifies you and marks you. Layered under both: **'Why can't anyone remember meeting me, why do the dates never change, and why is the switchboard operator the only person who won't call me Wren?'** The investigation braids three physical secrets — Gil's undelivered mailbag, the Registry basement beneath the exchange, and the 'hem' at the town's edge where a child once caught a living fish out of the sky — toward one document: a shutdown order for the entire town, signed in a hand the player has seen before. Their own.
protagonist: You are addressed as **Wren Vasco** — the town choirmaster and census-taker, home at last off the 11:47. Everyone in Foley knows you; no one can remember meeting you. The narration is Foley's collective voice ('Foley remembers you like this:') — which is itself a clue, because the town is literally remembering you into existence in real time. Character creation is diegetic: on your first night, neighbors at Cade's Diner reminisce — 'Wren, you remember how you always—' — and the player finishes their sentences in free text. Those answers become your stats and, unknowingly, your ammunition against yourself: in Act 3 the census reads your own words back as archival fact. The truth: you are not Wren. Wren recorded every soul in Foley and never recorded themselves, leaving a Wren-shaped hole in the archive — and when someone far away picked up the long-open line, the town poured them into that hole. The protagonist is the gap between player and character made playable: a vacancy wearing a name. Wren is not here. Neither, in the way Foley believes, are you. The character intuits this in flickers — hesitating at mirrors, flinching when Mercy says 'caller' — and the player's growing dread and the character's are the same dread, which is why the reveal lands as recognition rather than trick.
replayHook: Four stat archetypes produce genuinely different investigations (the Choirmaster hears the mystery, the Trespasser breaks into it, the Beloved is told it, the Static-heavy stranger is found by it — Pinch seeks YOU out), and Branch B means Act 3 plays against different surviving casts. On top: a **cross-run exchange logbook.** The game keeps a human-readable file, EXCHANGE_LOG.txt, beside the saves — no tricks, players can open it in any editor — and Mercy reads from it. Start NG+ and she says 'You again. The line remembers,' recalls your previous ending obliquely ('Some callers hang up. Some don't.'), and her trust carries a residue across runs; certain Act 3 dialogue only exists for players she has watched choose differently twice. NG+ also re-seeds 'Remembering Wren' — the town half-remembers your PREVIOUS answers and gently argues when you change them ('That's funny. I'd have sworn you hated rain'), turning replays into a conversation with your own past runs. Cross-platform is a replay engine too: one save code (a paper 'exchange ticket' the game prints) works in both builds, and playing the other platform is diegetic — 'you came in through the service entrance' — with corroborating information (Registry timestamps, marginalia) that rewards but never requires the trip. Completionists chase the jukebox: hearing all census tracks whole, including the four different resolutions of Track 00, requires most endings.
setting: **Foley** — a 1950s American mountain town of maybe three hundred souls, permanently at dusk, famous for its brass band and its telephone exchange. Warm porch lights, a diner with a jukebox, a bandstand on the square — and wrongness at the seams: rain you can hear but that never lands, dates on newspapers that never advance, and at the town's edge the streets dissolve into a 'hem' of static and the sound of deep water OVERHEAD. The truth of the place: Foley was condemned and drowned in June 1954 for the Cascade Reservoir. What persists is its archive — the Foley Sonic Census, a recording of every resident made before the flood — still running, still playing itself, seventy years on. The dual-platform requirement is the setting's anatomy, not a port: the **browser build is Foley as it renders itself** — illustrated, warm, fully scored, the lived town; the **terminal build is the Registry** — the same story experienced 'over the wire' through the exchange: transcripts, switchboard headers, telegraph rhythm, timestamps the browser politely fades out. The 1950s telephony aesthetic makes the terminal presentation diegetic rather than a downgrade — a teletype IS period technology — and either platform alone contains the full mystery; the other is corroboration, a second way of seeing the same drowned town.
statsDesign: Four stats, all created diegetically in the 'Remembering Wren' prologue — they are literally the shape of the hole the town pours you into. **REGARD** — how warmly Foley holds you: the town's social gravity. Gates which doors open without asking, whether residents shield you when Harrow chalks your door at Branch B, your seat at roll call, and whether Gil brings the mailbag to you or hides it. **ATTUNEMENT** — your ear for the census: the music pillar as mechanics. High Attunement widens the adaptive mix so you HEAR residents thinning days before anyone notices (an early-warning system), unlocks Steadying (humming someone's motif back to stabilize them), and reveals motif-quotations in the score that function as clues (e.g., hearing Odile's transpositions inside 'missing' parts). **TRESPASS** — your willingness to cross lines: opens sealed content (the Registry basement, Gil's floorboards, the hem at night) but every crossing is remembered even when unwitnessed — high Trespass makes Mercy formal, Gil evasive, and Harrow interested. **STATIC** — how much of the outside clings to you: the meta-stat, moved by play itself. Static rises when you do things no Foley resident would — refuse rituals, mention the world outside, and above all reload saves (every reload is a dropped call Mercy notices). High Static frightens ordinary residents but is the only thing Pinch trusts ('You buzz like the hem does') and makes Harrow flag you for assessment; low Static lets you pass as local. No strength, no charisma — every stat is a relationship between a town and a stranger, and every build (the Beloved, the Choirmaster, the Trespasser, the Ghost) opens a different route through Acts 2–3, which is the replay engine.
title: NOT HERE
twist: **Foley drowned on June 12, 1954.** The town was condemned for the Cascade Reservoir, and before the water came, choirmaster **Wren Vasco** conducted the Foley Sonic Census — recording every resident's voice and tune as an act of love, so the town would not simply vanish. The archive never stopped playing. Seventy years on, the recordings have been running so long they became people again, living one endless June inside the Registry, while the recordings themselves slowly degrade — that is the 'thinning,' and Mr. Harrow, the archive's deletion routine wearing the face of the county surveyor, chalks the unrecoverable so their corruption doesn't spread. The two-stage gut punch: **first**, you are not Wren. Wren recorded everyone and never recorded themselves — Track 00, [UNRECORDED] — leaving a hole in the archive shaped like its own maker. Mercy kept one line open out of the valley for seventy years, and when someone far away picked up — the player — the town poured them into the hole and called them home. Your character creation was the archive interviewing you to patch itself; your 'memories' are your own typed words read back. **Second**, the Final Disconnection Order that Harrow serves and Mercy refuses — the instruction to shut the archive down and let Foley end — is signed by Wren Vasco. The real Wren watched the recordings begin to suffer and, in a last act of the same love that made the census, ordered it ended… then died with the order unexecuted and a promise to Mercy unkept: 'I'll call when it's safe to hang up.' So the machinery of the game confesses itself: saves were Mercy holding your call, the terminal build was the wire, the browser build was the dream, and the question the whole game has been asking — 'where is Wren?' — has the same answer as the title. Not here. The only person who can sign, refuse, or rewrite that order is the stranger on the line: you.
whyNotUndertaleClone: We steal Undertale's principles — leitmotif as narrative load-bearing structure, a world that remembers, mechanics that confess to being mechanics — and none of its content or posture. **No combat, no mercy system, no morality meter dressed as gameplay:** the central verb is listening (Steadying, Attunement, the roll call), and the closest thing to a boss is a metronome. **The aware entity is a caretaker, not a gremlin:** Mercy never smirks at the fourth wall, never threatens your files, never performs 'I know you're playing a game.' She is a tired professional who has held a line open for seventy years, and her awareness of the player is the tenderest thing in the story rather than its jump-scare — the meta register is grief and telephony, not horror and glitch (contra DDLC), and the player is a long-distance caller, not a god or a savior (contra OneShot). **The meta layer is infrastructural, not intrusive:** saves, the cross-run logbook, and above all the dual-platform requirement — browser as the town's dream of itself, terminal as the wire it survives on — a story element no single-build game could have, and one that stays optional: either platform alone carries the whole mystery. **Memory is social fabric, not judgment:** the game never grades you; the town just holds you the way you taught it to, and the consequences arrive as changed arrangements in the score and changed grammar in people's mouths, not as a judgment hall. And where Undertale's genocide route weaponizes completionism, our darkest path (Survey Complete) is reached by ordinary triage logic and small reasonable bargains — the horror of being agreeable, which is a different, older kind of chill.
